title: Bari

infobox.value: ([/ˈbɑːri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*BAR-ee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), Italian: [[ˈbaːri]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Bari.ogg "File:It-Bari.ogg"); [Barese](/wiki/Barese_dialect "Barese dialect"): *Bare* [[ˈbæːrə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Neapolitan "Help:IPA/Neapolitan"); [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Barium*) is the capital city of the [Metropolitan City of Bari](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Bari "Metropolitan City of Bari") and of the [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia") region, on the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea") in [southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy"). It is one of the most important economic centres of mainland southern Italy. It is a [port](/wiki/Port "Port") and [university](/wiki/University "University") city as well as the city of [Saint Nicholas](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas "Saint Nicholas"). The city itself has a population of 315,473 inhabitants, and an area of over 116 square kilometres (45 mi2), while the urban area has approximately 750,000 inhabitants. Its [metropolitan province](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Bari "Metropolitan City of Bari") has 1.2 million inhabitants.[[2]](#cite_note-population-2)

**Bari** ([/ˈbɑːri/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*BAR-ee*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"), Italian: [[ˈbaːri]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Italian "Help:IPA/Italian") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:It-Bari.ogg "File:It-Bari.ogg"); [Barese](/wiki/Barese_dialect "Barese dialect"): *Bare* [[ˈbæːrə]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Neapolitan "Help:IPA/Neapolitan"); [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Barium*) is the capital city of the [Metropolitan City of Bari](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Bari "Metropolitan City of Bari") and of the [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia") region, on the [Adriatic Sea](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea") in [southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy"). It is one of the most important economic centres of mainland southern Italy. It is a [port](/wiki/Port "Port") and [university](/wiki/University "University") city as well as the city of [Saint Nicholas](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas "Saint Nicholas"). The city itself has a population of 315,473 inhabitants, and an area of over 116 square kilometres (45 mi2), while the urban area has approximately 750,000 inhabitants. Its [metropolitan province](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Bari "Metropolitan City of Bari") has 1.2 million inhabitants.[[2]](#cite_note-population-2)

Bari is made up of four different urban sections. To the north is the closely built old town on the peninsula between two modern harbours, with the [Basilica of Saint Nicholas](/wiki/Basilica_di_San_Nicola "Basilica di San Nicola"), the [Cathedral of San Sabino](/wiki/Cathedral_of_San_Sabino "Cathedral of San Sabino") (1035–1171) and the [Norman-Swabian Castle](/wiki/Castello_Normanno-Svevo_(Bari) "Castello Normanno-Svevo (Bari)"), which is now also a major nightlife district. To the south is the Murat quarter (erected by [Joachim Murat](/wiki/Joachim_Murat "Joachim Murat")), the modern heart of the city, which is laid out on a rectangular grid-plan with a [promenade](/wiki/Promenade "Promenade") on the sea and the major shopping district (the *via Sparano* and *via Argiro*).

Modern residential zones surrounding the centre of Bari were built during the 1960s and 1970s replacing the old suburbs that had developed along roads splaying outwards from gates in the city walls. In addition, the outer suburbs developed rapidly during the 1990s.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bari&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

For a chronological guide, see [Timeline of Bari](/wiki/Timeline_of_Bari "Timeline of Bari").

### Ancient

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bari&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ancient")]

Bari itself known in antiquity as *Barium*, was a harbour of the [Iapygian](/wiki/Iapygians "Iapygians") [Peuceti](/wiki/Peuceti "Peuceti").[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) The authors of the [Etymologicum Magnum](/wiki/Etymologicum_Magnum "Etymologicum Magnum") have preserved an etymology by authors of antiquity about *Barium*, which they explain as the word "house" in [Messapic](/wiki/Messapic_language "Messapic language").[[6]](#cite_note-6) The city had strong Greek influences before the Roman era.[[7]](#cite_note-7) In [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek"), it was known as *Βάριον*. In the 3rd century BC, it became part of the [Roman Republic](/wiki/Roman_Republic "Roman Republic") and was subsequently Romanized. The city developed strategic significance as the point of junction between the coast road and the [Via Traiana](/wiki/Via_Traiana "Via Traiana") and as a port for eastward trade; a branch road to [Tarentum](/wiki/Taranto "Taranto") led from Barium. Its harbour, mentioned as early as 181 BC, was probably the principal one of the districts in ancient times, as it is at present, and was the centre of a fishery.[[8]](#cite_note-EB1911-8) The first historical bishop of [Bari](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Bari "Bishopric of Bari") was [Gervasius](/wiki/Gervasius_and_Protasius "Gervasius and Protasius") who was noted at the [Council of Sardica](/wiki/Council_of_Sardica "Council of Sardica") in 347.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bari&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

With the [fall of the Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Western_Roman_Empire "Fall of the Western Roman Empire") in 476, Bari was invaded by [Barbarians](/wiki/Barbarian "Barbarian") and occupied by the [Ostrogoths](/wiki/Ostrogoths "Ostrogoths"). It was taken from them by the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") during the [Gothic wars](/wiki/Gothic_wars "Gothic wars") and disputed for the following two centuries with the [Lombards](/wiki/Lombards "Lombards") of the [Duchy of Benevento](/wiki/Duchy_of_Benevento "Duchy of Benevento"), who made it a steward.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Throughout this period, and indeed throughout the Middle Ages, Bari served as one of the major [slave](/wiki/Slavery "Slavery") depots of the Mediterranean, providing a central location for the trade in [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_peoples "Slavic peoples") slaves.[[11]](#cite_note-11) The slaves were mostly captured by [Venice](/wiki/Republic_of_Venice "Republic of Venice") from [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia"), by the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") from what is now [Eastern Germany](/wiki/Eastern_germany "Eastern germany") and [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland"), and by the Byzantines from elsewhere in the Balkans, and were generally destined for other parts of the Byzantine Empire and (most frequently) the Muslim states surrounding the Mediterranean: the [Abbasid Caliphate](/wiki/Abbasid_Caliphate "Abbasid Caliphate"), the [Umayyad](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") [Caliphate of Córdoba](/wiki/Caliphate_of_C%C3%B3rdoba "Caliphate of Córdoba"), the [Emirate of Sicily](/wiki/Emirate_of_Sicily "Emirate of Sicily"), and the [Fatimid Caliphate](/wiki/Fatimid_Caliphate "Fatimid Caliphate") (which relied on Slavs purchased at the Bari market for its legions of Sakalaba [Mamluks](/wiki/Mamluk "Mamluk")).[[12]](#cite_note-12)

For 20 years, Bari was the centre of the [Emirate of Bari](/wiki/Emirate_of_Bari "Emirate of Bari"); the city was captured by its first emirs [Kalfun](/wiki/Kalfun "Kalfun") in 847, who had been part of the mercenary garrison installed there by [Radelchis I of Benevento](/wiki/Radelchis_I_of_Benevento "Radelchis I of Benevento").[[13]](#cite_note-Wickham62-13) The city was conquered and the emirate extinguished in 871 following [five-year campaign](/wiki/Louis_II%27s_campaign_against_Bari_(866%E2%80%93871) "Louis II's campaign against Bari (866–871)") by [Frankish Emperor Louis II](/wiki/Emperor_Louis_II "Emperor Louis II"), assisted by a [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") fleet.[[14]](#cite_note-14) [Chris Wickham](/wiki/Chris_Wickham "Chris Wickham") states Louis spent five years campaigning to reduce then occupy Bari, "and then only to a Byzantine/Slav naval blockade"; "Louis took the credit" for the success, adding "at least in Frankish eyes", then concludes by noting that by remaining in southern Italy long after this success, he "achieved the near-impossible: an alliance against him of the Beneventans, Salernitans, Neapolitans and Spoletans; later sources include [Sawadān](/wiki/Sawdan "Sawdan") as well."[[13]](#cite_note-Wickham62-13) In 885, Bari became the residence of the local Byzantine *[catapan](/wiki/Catapan "Catapan")*, or governor. The failed revolt (1009–1011) of the Lombard nobles [Melus of Bari](/wiki/Melus_of_Bari "Melus of Bari") and his [brother-in-law](/wiki/Brother-in-law "Brother-in-law") Dattus, against the Byzantine governorate, though it was firmly repressed at the [Battle of Cannae (1018)](/wiki/Battle_of_Cannae_(1018) "Battle of Cannae (1018)"), offered their [Norman](/wiki/Normans "Normans") adventurer allies a first foothold in the region.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In 1025, under the [Archbishop Byzantius](/wiki/Byzantius_(archbishop_of_Bari) "Byzantius (archbishop of Bari)"), Bari became attached to the [see of Rome](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") and was granted "[provincial](/wiki/Province "Province")" status.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

In 1071, Bari was captured by [Robert Guiscard](/wiki/Robert_Guiscard "Robert Guiscard"), following a [three-year siege](/wiki/Siege_of_Bari "Siege of Bari"), ending what remained of the Byzantine power in the region.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Following this, Bari's physical and political landscapes were changed: the *praetorium* which had functioned as a political centre was converted into the [Basilica of Saint Nicholas](/wiki/Basilica_of_Saint_Nicholas,_Bari "Basilica of Saint Nicholas, Bari"). Relics of [Saint Nicholas](/wiki/Saint_Nicholas "Saint Nicholas") were brought to Bari in 1087 and installed in the basilica.[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17)

In 1095, [Peter the Hermit](/wiki/Peter_the_Hermit "Peter the Hermit") [preached](/wiki/Preaching "Preaching") the [First Crusade](/wiki/First_Crusade "First Crusade") there.[[8]](#cite_note-EB1911-8) In October 1098, [Urban II](/wiki/Urban_II "Urban II"), who had consecrated the Basilica in 1089, convened the [Council of Bari](/wiki/Council_of_Bari "Council of Bari"), one of a series of [synods](/wiki/Synod "Synod") convoked with the intention of reconciling the [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Church "Eastern Church") and [Western Church](/wiki/Western_Church "Western Church") on the question of the [filioque](/wiki/Filioque "Filioque") clause in the Creed, which [Anselm](/wiki/Anselm_of_Canterbury "Anselm of Canterbury") ably defended, seated at the pope's side.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In 1136 the Holy Roman Emperor, [Lothair](/wiki/Lothair_III,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Lothair III, Holy Roman Emperor"), captured Bari.[[19]](#cite_note-19) A Byzantine army took control of Bari from [William I of Sicily](/wiki/William_I_of_Sicily "William I of Sicily") in 1155. William recaptured the city the following year, and on finding that his house had been destroyed began a campaign of reprisals causing widespread destruction across the city. The city walls were destroyed, an act which archaeologist Giulia Bellato suggests was a symbolic downgrading of Bari's status and that of its inhabitants. The events are chronicled by [Hugo Falcandus](/wiki/Hugo_Falcandus "Hugo Falcandus").[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17)

During [Joanna I of Naples](/wiki/Joanna_I_of_Naples "Joanna I of Naples")' conflict with Hungary, Bari was besieged in 1349 and captured by a combined Germany and Hungarian army.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Early modern period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bari&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Early modern period")]

Main article: [Duchy of Bari](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bari "Duchy of Bari")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/XIX_century_print%2C_Il_porto_di_Bari.jpg/250px-XIX_century_print%2C_Il_porto_di_Bari.jpg)](/wiki/File:XIX_century_print,_Il_porto_di_Bari.jpg)

19th-century image of the port of Bari

A long period of decline characterized the city under the dominations of [Aldoino Filangieri di Candida](/wiki/Aldoino_Filangieri_di_Candida "Aldoino Filangieri di Candida"), and those of the [Kings of Naples](/wiki/Kings_of_Naples "Kings of Naples"), which held the control of the entire mainland southern Italy from 1282 to 1806. This decline was interrupted, however, by the splendor under the [Sforzas](/wiki/Sforzas "Sforzas"), who ruled the city as [Dukes of Bari](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bari "Duchy of Bari"), a title given by the Neapolitan crown, in particular under the rule of the dukes [Ludovico](/wiki/Ludovico_Sforza "Ludovico Sforza") and [Beatrice d'Este](/wiki/Beatrice_d%27Este "Beatrice d'Este"), then of the duchesses [Isabella of Aragon](/wiki/Isabella_of_Aragon,_Duchess_of_Milan "Isabella of Aragon, Duchess of Milan") and [Bona Sforza](/wiki/Bona_Sforza "Bona Sforza"). Bari also underwent Venetian domination, which led to the expansion of the port and a very prosperous period, also favored by the trade of inland products, which were in great demand on foreign markets.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-:0-22)

In 1556, Princess Bona Sforza of Aragon, second wife of the [King of Poland Sigismund I](/wiki/Sigismund_I_the_Old "Sigismund I the Old"), left Poland and settled in Bari, whose principality she had inherited from her parents. During her reign, she fortified the city's castle, as evidenced by an inscription in bronze letters on the cornice around the courtyard, as well as building several churches, a monastery, two water cisterns and made many donations to the monks of the Basilica of San Nicola. Bona Sforza died in the city in 1557.[[22]](#cite_note-:0-22) Following her death, the city of Bari came under the direct rule of the kings of Naples.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In 1813, [Joachim Murat](/wiki/Joachim_Murat "Joachim Murat"), [King of Naples](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Naples_(Napoleonic) "Kingdom of Naples (Napoleonic)") in the [Napoleonic era](/wiki/Napoleonic_era "Napoleonic era"), began a new urbanization, changing the face of the city and setting a new "chessboard" growth model, which continued for many years to come. The village built at the time on the outskirts of the old city still retains its name.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Modern plumbing arrived in the city of Bari on 24 April 1915: it was the first completed leg of the nascent Apulian Aqueduct. During the 1930s, [Araldo di Crollalanza](/wiki/Araldo_di_Crollalanza "Araldo di Crollalanza"), the mayor and minister of Bari, oversaw the development of its modern waterfront.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bari&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: World War II")]

On 11 September 1943, in connection with the [Armistice of Cassibile](/wiki/Armistice_of_Cassibile "Armistice of Cassibile"), Bari was taken without resistance by the [British 1st Airborne Division](/wiki/1st_Airborne_Division_(United_Kingdom) "1st Airborne Division (United Kingdom)"),[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] then during October and November 1943, New Zealand troops from the 2nd New Zealand Division assembled in Bari.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

#### The 1943 chemical warfare disaster

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bari&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: The 1943 chemical warfare disaster")]

See also: [Air raid on Bari](/wiki/Air_raid_on_Bari "Air raid on Bari")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/4/40/SS_John_Harvey_on_fire_12-2-1943%2C_Italian_port_of_Bari.JPG)](/wiki/File:SS_John_Harvey_on_fire_12-2-1943,_Italian_port_of_Bari.JPG)

The SS *John Harvey* on fire  
on 2 December 1943, at Bari

A fleet of German [Luftwaffe](/wiki/Luftwaffe "Luftwaffe") bombers attacked Allied forces and shipping in Bari on 2 December 1943. An American ship, the [SS *John Harvey*](/wiki/SS_John_Harvey "SS John Harvey"), carrying a secret cargo of 2,000 [mustard gas](/wiki/Mustard_gas "Mustard gas") bombs, was destroyed causing release of the gas into the air and sea, resulting in death, chemical burns, and blindness among sailors and the civilian population of the city.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

A member of U.S. General [Dwight D. Eisenhower](/wiki/Dwight_D._Eisenhower "Dwight D. Eisenhower")'s medical staff, Stewart F. Alexander, was dispatched to Bari following the raid. Alexander had trained at the Army's [Edgewood Arsenal](/wiki/Edgewood_Arsenal "Edgewood Arsenal") in Maryland,[[27]](#cite_note-27)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] and was familiar with some of the effects of mustard gas. Although he was not informed of the cargo carried by the *John Harvey*, and most victims suffered atypical symptoms caused by exposure to mustard diluted in water and oil (as opposed to airborne), Alexander rapidly concluded that mustard gas was present. Although he could not get any acknowledgement of this from the chain of command, Alexander convinced medical staffs to treat patients for mustard exposure and saved many lives as a result. He also preserved many tissue samples from autopsied victims at Bari. After World War II, these samples would result in the development of an early form of [chemotherapy](/wiki/Chemotherapy "Chemotherapy") based on mustard, [Mustine](/wiki/Mustine "Mustine").[[28]](#cite_note-Infield1976-28)

On the orders of Allied leaders [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt"), [Winston Churchill](/wiki/Winston_Churchill "Winston Churchill"), and Eisenhower, records were destroyed and the whole affair was kept secret for many years after the war. The U.S. records of the attack were declassified in 1959, but the episode remained obscure until 1967, when writer Glenn B. Infield exposed the story in his book *Disaster at Bari*.[[28]](#cite_note-Infield1976-28) Additionally, there is considerable dispute as to the exact number of fatalities. In one account: "[S]ixty-nine deaths were attributed in whole or in part to the mustard gas, most of them American merchant seamen."[[29]](#cite_note-29) Others put the count as high as "more than one thousand Allied servicemen and more than one thousand Italian civilians".[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The affair is the subject of two books: the aforementioned *Disaster at Bari*, by Glenn B. Infield, and *Nightmare in Bari: The World War II Liberty Ship Poison Gas Disaster and Coverup*, by [Gerald Reminick](/w/index.php?title=Gerald_Reminick&action=edit&redlink=1 "Gerald Reminick (page does not exist)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In 1988, through the efforts of [Nick T. Spark](/wiki/Nick_T._Spark "Nick T. Spark"), U.S. Senators [Dennis DeConcini](/wiki/Dennis_DeConcini "Dennis DeConcini") and [Bill Bradley](/wiki/Bill_Bradley "Bill Bradley"), Stewart Alexander received recognition from the [Surgeon General of the United States Army](/wiki/Surgeon_General_of_the_United_States_Army "Surgeon General of the United States Army") for his actions during the Bari disaster.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

#### *Charles Henderson* explosion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bari&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Charles Henderson explosion")]

The port of Bari was again struck by disaster on 9 April 1945 when the Liberty ship *[Charles Henderson](/wiki/SS_Charles_Henderson "SS Charles Henderson")* exploded in the harbour while offloading 2,000 tons of aerial bombs (half of that amount had been offloaded when the explosion occurred). Three hundred and sixty people were killed and 1,730 were wounded. The harbour was again rendered non-operational, this time for a month.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bari&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Geography")]

Bari is the largest urban and metro area on the [Adriatic](/wiki/Adriatic_Sea "Adriatic Sea"). It is located in [southern Italy](/wiki/Southern_Italy "Southern Italy"), at a more northerly latitude than [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples"), further south than [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome").

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bari&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Climate")]

Bari has a hot-summer [Mediterranean climate](/wiki/Mediterranean_climate "Mediterranean climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Csa*) bordering on a [cold semi-arid climate](/wiki/Cold_semi-arid_climate "Cold semi-arid climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): BSk) due to high evapotranspiration, with mild winters and hot, dry summers.

### Migration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bari&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Migration")]

According to an urban migration study in Bari, return migration gain to urban areas is higher than migration loss from urban areas. People migrating from urban destinations tend to migrate to different places in comparison to people migrating from rural areas. These findings are based on the background and behavior of a sample of 211 return migrants to Bari, Italy. Bari is a port city, making it historically important because of its strong trade links with Greece, North Africa, and the eastern Mediterranean. Bari's economic structure is based on industry, commerce, services, and administration. Around two-thirds of the city's employment is in the tertiary sector with its port, commerce, and administrative functions. The highest percentage of Bari's working population is employed in services, with 45.6%. From 1958 to 1982, around 20% of migrants left Bari for other Italian communes, while around 17% or migrants came to Bari from other Italian communes. Under 2% of migrants left Bari to go abroad and came to the city from abroad.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bari&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6a/Bari_Central_Station.jpg/250px-Bari_Central_Station.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bari_Central_Station.jpg)

Bari Central Station

Bari has its own airport, [Bari Karol Wojtyła Airport](/wiki/Bari_Karol_Wojty%C5%82a_Airport "Bari Karol Wojtyła Airport"), which is located 8 km (5.0 mi) northwest of the centre of Bari. It is connected to the centre by train services from [Bari Aeroporto railway station](/wiki/Bari_Aeroporto_railway_station "Bari Aeroporto railway station").

[Bari Central Station](/wiki/Bari_Centrale_railway_station "Bari Centrale railway station") lies on the [Adriatic railway](/wiki/Adriatic_railway "Adriatic railway") and has connections to cities such as [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), [Bologna](/wiki/Bologna "Bologna"), [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin") and [Venice](/wiki/Venice "Venice"). Another mainline is connected southwards by the [Bari-Taranto railway](/wiki/Bari-Taranto_railway "Bari-Taranto railway"). The [Bari metropolitan railway service](/wiki/Bari_metropolitan_railway_service "Bari metropolitan railway service") operates local commuter services, while regional services also operate to [Foggia](/wiki/Foggia "Foggia"), [Barletta](/wiki/Barletta "Barletta"), [Brindisi](/wiki/Brindisi "Brindisi"), [Lecce](/wiki/Lecce "Lecce"), [Taranto](/wiki/Taranto "Taranto") and other towns and villages in the Apulia region.

Bari has an old fishery port (*Porto Vecchio*) and a so-called new port in the north, as well as some marinas. The Port of Bari is an important cargo transport hub to [Southeast Europe](/wiki/Southeast_Europe "Southeast Europe"). Various passenger transport lines include some seasonal ferry lines to [Albania](/wiki/Albania "Albania"), [Montenegro](/wiki/Montenegro "Montenegro") or [Dubrovnik](/wiki/Dubrovnik "Dubrovnik"). Bari – [Igoumenitsa](/wiki/Igoumenitsa "Igoumenitsa") is a popular ferry route to Greece. Some cruise ships call at Bari.

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bari&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

The Guido Guerrieri novels by [Gianrico Carofiglio](/wiki/Gianrico_Carofiglio "Gianrico Carofiglio") are set in Bari, where Guerrieri is a criminal lawyer, and include many descriptions of the town.

Bari is one of the primary settings of the 1954 detective novel *[The Black Mountain](/wiki/The_Black_Mountain_(novel) "The Black Mountain (novel)")* by [Rex Stout](/wiki/Rex_Stout "Rex Stout"). It is the characters' point of embarkation to [Communist Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia").

In the 1995 film *[The Bridges of Madison County](/wiki/The_Bridges_of_Madison_County_(film) "The Bridges of Madison County (film)")*, Italian housewife Francesca Johnson ([Meryl Streep](/wiki/Meryl_Streep "Meryl Streep")), is mentioned as being from Bari and growing up in Naples.

The 2020 Edoardo Ponti film *[La vita davanti a sé](/wiki/The_Life_Ahead "The Life Ahead")*, starring [Sophia Loren](/wiki/Sophia_Loren "Sophia Loren"), is set in Bari.

Two Italian TV series -- *[Lolita Lobosco](/w/index.php?title=Lolita_Lobosco&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lolita Lobosco (page does not exist)")* and *[Cold Summer](/wiki/Cold_Summer "Cold Summer")* -- are set in Bari.
